reviews.text:
Been a while since I worked with Lauren on my custody/divorce case. The resolution wasn't great. To be clear I have no drug records, no criminal history, am employed full time with a solid, lucrative career. There have been zero cases brought against me ever. My ex tricked me into signing a separation agreement that granted him full custody, then he moved my son an hour away and left me with very few options.

When I called Lauren and explained the situation she seemed to believe that we could very easily regain joint custody and deal with the commute that was forced upon me to visit my kid.

During the course of my case, I probably had to repeat the same information to Lauren 20 times. None of the information that I relayed during our phone screen stuck, I had to repeat all of it.

When it came time to start drafting paperwork, Lauren was so all over the place and hard to reach that my ex said to me with a smug tone, "I think we should have my lawyer draft this up, yours seems to be a little spacey" - Extremely embarrassing.

I ended up with no custody + paying child support. They didn't have to move my kid back or make any concessions for travel. I got visitation, but not a lot of it. And after I REALLY pushed her to get it done, I got a promise that they won't move him FURTHER away. Again, I had to really push her to try and get that done.

She sort of just let them make all the offers and then showed it to me and / I / was the one who had to be like, no, that's not fair, we should push to change it.

Like, she was essentially no more than a mouthpiece.

It's not like she was cheap. I had to pay her a $5,000 retainer.

This was a couple years ago now, so I've given myself plenty of time to cool off.

After deciding to switch attorneys, my final invoice showed I was billed for everything: reading emails, thinking about emails, possibly dreaming about emails — all at premium hourly rates. Nearly half the bill was labeled as “correspondence” and vague entries like “review papers” and “update file,” which is impressively unhelpful for something costing $300/hour.

Nothing motivates detailed billing quite like a client firing you.

To be fair, they were prompt, professional, and efficient — especially when it came to draining the retainer on the way out the door.

If you enjoy mystery charges, aggressive time rounding, and paying luxury prices for routine tasks, you’ll feel right at home.
reviews.ownerResponse:
I disagree with your characterization of the billing in this matter.

Your case was handled pursuant to a written flat-fee agreement, which you signed. That agreement expressly provided that if the representation is terminated, the fair value of services performed is calculated at the agreed hourly rate—which is competitive in this market. After you elected to retain new counsel, a final accounting was completed in accordance with that agreement and the remaining balance was refunded.

Because attorneys are bound by rules of confidentiality, I can't publicly discuss the specifics of a former client’s matter. I can say that the time reflected on the invoice corresponds to work actually performed, including review of materials, legal research, communications, and case preparation.

We strive to provide professional and transparent representation to every client, and I stand by the services rendered in your case.

So I ended up having to pay $400 to the Broome DA office and take a defensive driving course anyways, and Ms. Murphy and Off the Record refused to refund my $150 even though their "services" added literally 0 value. The only "work" her office did was  fax over a templated letter over to the DA indicating a not guilty plea. Long story short, save your money and just do the traffic diversion program directly.

reviews.ownerResponse:
Dear Mr. Curry – It seems you misunderstood and misconstrued what was stated to you. We regularly represent “the little man” against larger entities. However, like ALL attorneys and law firms, there are some practice areas and case types we simply do not handle, such as debtor’s rights, for example. Some firms only handle debtors' rights cases, and do not represent creditors, for example. While maybe it all seems “the same” to you, the work different attorneys and law firms do can be very different. ALL attorneys and law firms have certain practice areas and case types they handle and don’t handle.

The particular nature of your case fell into a category we don’t handle. We also have to abide by ethical rules, avoid conflicts of interest, and we often can’t disclose the full extent of those. To ensure compliance with ethical rules and also to not waste your time, we declined to listen to the details of your case. Just because we’re not representing a party with respect to your particular case, doesn’t mean we’re not working or engaging with them in other capacities.

Unfortunately, we are not able to accept every matter that is presented to us, and your matter was unique. We’re not sure why you’d be upset that we were honest with you about the case types we regularly handle, so that you could find an attorney who regularly handles the unique case type you were calling about.

We wish you all the best,
-C&G

reviews.ownerResponse:
Mr. Wescott,
While we disagree with your characterization of what occurred in this matter, we care very deeply about our clients and are always looking for ways to improve. Our records show that we successfully obtained the necessary judicial approval of the $100,000 settlement in June of 2017, at which time the entire matter was carefully reviewed by a New York State Supreme Court justice who approved a $25,000 fee to cover the more than 80 hours of legal work which we performed on behalf of your son. As you may know, there are special legal requirements that must be satisfied when handling a personal injury matter for a child and it requires a significant amount of time together with a special amount of legal knowledge and experience in order to properly satisfy those requirements. In this case, the judge decided that the amount of time we worked on the matter was totally appropriate and that all of those requirements were properly satisfied, but we recognize that you disagree with that decision.

Their knowledge and understanding of the situation and the best course of action was precise and clear.  They informed us of every step along the way and even knew what issues we would encounter before it happened.

Shortly after the accident my father received a bill for nearly $30,000 from the hospital and emergency room.  I immediately forwarded the bill to Jan, and she got the bill to the proper responsible party to be paid.  Kevin and Jan were able to get all the bills paid and even corrected a bill that was paid in error by my father's insurance carrier.

My parents ended up with no out of packet payments for any of the many doctor visits or tests, and received a very nice reward from the driver's insurance company.  The outcome was better than I ever expected.  However, what I appreciated the most was the care and concern both Kevin and Jan had for my parents’ well being, the effort they gave, and timeliness of the entire process.

This review is 20 years too late. I engaged this law firm to represent me in a Labor and Employment issue that dealt with an age discrimination complaint. My case was investigated by a EEO Civil Right Division review process as a federal employee and as the plaintiff. The law firm did advise me the report did conclude and establish favorable "prima facie" standard as evidence was sufficient to establish a fact that discrimination did occur. I believe I was told by the law firm my case did get assigned to the EEOC in Washington DC. The law firm advised me they would not go forward with my complaint and wanted to me settle the case. Reluctantly I did at my own misfortune. Here is where I made my mistake. One: I did not ask for a copy of the report. Two: I did not get a second opinion. Third: The law firm did not as a courtesy give me a copy of the report voluntarily. This past week I contacted the law firm and request a copy on 3 attempts. My calls were never returned. I will now reach out to other entities and see if I get results. My advice to anyone wanting to get representation in the area Labor and Employment law-is to look elsewhere but not in the Binghamton area. Binghamton is a small town (with no future) with small town law firms whose only objective is to take on "slam dunk " cases. This law firm lacks the courage, compassion and commitment to fight for the plaintiffs like me. This reminds me of the movie Philadelphia where Denzel Washinton character as the attorney had the courage to pursuit the case on behalf of Tom Hanks character. You know how the movie ends. I have many regrets about engaging this firm- but it will take larger much larger law firms from cities like NYC or Washinton DC to get the courage, compassion and commitment you will need. I only have myself to blame

In my work as a real estate professional—broker, investor, and redeveloper—Angelina became my trusted attorney for both personal real estate transactions and client real estate representation. She was exceptional to work with and particularly effective alongside professionals who are direct, diligent, thorough, and results-oriented. She was consistently accessible, highly responsive, and proactive, which inspired complete confidence at every stage of a deal.

She handled transactions ranging from straightforward, all-cash, non-contingent deals to highly complex, structured transactions with multiple moving parts, including portfolio financing, hard-money investments, and redevelopment projects. She worked effectively with attorneys for all parties involved, consistently protecting her clients’ interests through strong due diligence, sound judgment, and strategic advocacy.

Prior to her retirement, Angelina took the time to send a personal email to me. Her message was thoughtful and meaningful, especially coming from a true giant in the real estate legal profession, and it reflected the same professionalism, clarity, and respect she consistently demonstrated throughout her career.

First, this law firm's performance has significantly declined over the past twelve years. I specifically requested Xander to handle my foreclosure/bankruptcy case since he was the lawyer who represented me when I first purchased the home twelve years ago. Initially, Xander showed concern when I mentioned receiving photocopied bank statements and asked me to bring them in for review. I visited the firm on June 12th to meet with Xander, but instead, I was handed off to an assistant. I was informed that a different attorney would handle my case and that the attorney I was supposed to meet had chosen to go to Oneonta that day instead. The assistant, who had no knowledge of foreclosure/bankruptcy cases, made some photocopies, making the trip a waste of time for my father and me.

On July 3rd, I received a call from another assistant named Darcy. She mentioned that an attorney named Bill Casey had called and talked to me about my case, which was untrue. No one had called me until July 3rd, and it was the assistant. She also stated that they couldn't handle my case as the firm does not handle bankruptcies/foreclosures despite their involvement in real estate transactions. Darcy admitted that although the firm couldn’t help me, they were still interested in the status of my bankruptcy/foreclosure. This admission indicates that the firm accessed my confidential information not to assist me but to gather information for their purposes, which is highly illegal. This is the second law firm caught engaging in such practices, the first being Coughlin and Gerhardt, which I also reviewed negatively on Google and Facebook.
